content: This Act may be called the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939. It extends to all the provinces and the Capital of the Federation.

section: Section 2 - Grounds for decree of dissolution of marriage
content: A woman married under Muslim Law is entitled to a decree dissolving her marriage on one or more of the following grounds: (i) the husband's whereabouts have been unknown for four years; (ii) the husband has neglected or failed to provide maintenance for two years; (ii-A) the husband has taken an additional wife in contravention of the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961; (iii) the husband has been sentenced to imprisonment for seven years or more; (iv) the husband has failed, without reasonable cause, to perform his marital obligations for three years; (v) the husband was impotent at the time of marriage and continues to be so; (vi) the husband has been insane for two years or suffers from leprosy or a virulent venereal disease; (vii) she was given in marriage by her father or guardian before age sixteen and repudiated the marriage before age eighteen, provided it was not consummated; (viii) the husband treats her with cruelty, including habitual assault, associating with women of evil repute, attempting to force her into an immoral life, disposing of her property or preventing her from exercising legal rights over it, obstructing her religious practice, or, if he has multiple wives, not treating her equitably per the Quran; (ix) any other ground recognized as valid under Muslim Law. A decree on ground (i) does not take effect for six months, during which the husband may appear and satisfy the Court he is prepared to resume conjugal duties, in which case the decree is set aside. Before a decree on ground (v), the Court gives the husband one year to prove he is no longer impotent.

section: Section 3 - Notice to heirs when husband's whereabouts unknown
content: In a suit under ground (i) of Section 2, the names and addresses of persons who would be the husband's heirs under Muslim Law (had he died on the date of filing) must be stated in the plaint, notice of the suit must be served on them, and they have the right to be heard. The husband's paternal uncle and brother, if any, must be cited as parties even if not heirs.

section: Section 4 - Effect of conversion to another faith
content: A married Muslim woman's renunciation of Islam, or her conversion to another faith, does not by itself dissolve her marriage. However, after such renunciation or conversion, she is entitled to seek dissolution on any ground in Section 2. This does not apply to a woman who converted to Islam from another faith and later re-embraces her former faith.

section: Section 5 - Rights to dower not affected
content: Nothing in this Act affects any right a married woman may have under Muslim law to her dower, or any part of it, upon dissolution of her marriage.
